3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Store anything. Access from anywhere. Never lose a file again.

Welcome Back
In Episode 1 we learned what AWS is. In Episode 2 we launched our first EC2 server.
Today we learn S3 — the storage service you’ll use in almost every real project you ever build.
Let’s go.

What is S3?
S3 stands for Simple Storage Service.
Here’s the simplest explanation:
S3 is like Google Drive — but built for developers and applications.
You can store any file. Images. Videos. PDFs. Code files. Database backups. Anything. And access it from anywhere in the world through a URL.
The things you store in S3 are called objects. The container that holds objects is called a bucket.
That’s it. Objects in buckets. Simple.

Why S3 is Used Everywhere
Every real application uses S3 for something.

• User profile pictures → stored in S3

• Product images on e-commerce sites → stored in S3

• Video files on streaming platforms → stored in S3

• Database backups → stored in S3

• Static website files → hosted on S3
AWS guarantees 99.999999999% durability — that’s 11 nines. Your files are safer in S3 than on any hard drive you own.

Create Your First S3 Bucket
Step 1 — Open S3 Console
AWS Console → Search “S3” → Click S3
Step 2 — Create Bucket
Click “Create bucket”

• Bucket name: must be globally unique — try “yourname-first-bucket-2026”

• Region: ap-south-1 (Mumbai)

• Keep all default settings

• Click “Create bucket”
Step 3 — Upload a File
Click your bucket → Click “Upload” → Add any file → Click Upload
Your file is now stored in the cloud. Accessible from anywhere.

Access Your Files
Click on your uploaded file. You’ll see an Object URL.
It looks like:
https://your-bucket.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/yourfile.jpgBy default this URL won’t work publicly — your bucket is private.
To make one file publicly accessible:

• Click the file → Permissions → Edit → Make public
Now anyone with that URL can access your file.

S3 with Python — The Real Power
This is where S3 becomes truly useful for developers.
import boto3# Connect to S3
s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='ap-south-1')# Upload a file
s3.upload_file(
'local-file.txt', # File on your computer
'your-bucket-name', # Your S3 bucket
'uploaded-file.txt' # Name in S3
)
print("Uploaded successfully!")# Download a file
s3.download_file(
'your-bucket-name', # Your S3 bucket
'uploaded-file.txt', # File in S3
'downloaded-file.txt' # Save as this name locally
)
print("Downloaded successfully!")# List all files in bucket
response = s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket='your-bucket-name')
for obj in response['Contents']:
print(f"File: {obj['Key']} | Size: {obj['Size']} bytes")Three operations. Upload. Download. List. These cover 90% of what you’ll ever do with S3.

S3 Free Tier
For beginners — completely free within these limits:

• 5 GB storage — first 12 months

• 20,000 GET requests per month

• 2,000 PUT requests per month
For learning and small projects — more than enough.

Common Mistakes to Avoid
Mistake 1 — Making entire bucket public Never make your whole bucket public. Only make specific files public when needed.
Mistake 2 — Storing credentials in code Never hardcode AWS keys. Always use environment variables.
Mistake 3 — Wrong region Always use ap-south-1 for India. Faster for Indian users.

Quick Reference
import boto3
s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='ap-south-1')# Upload
s3.upload_file('local.txt', 'bucket-name', 's3-key.txt')# Download
s3.download_file('bucket-name', 's3-key.txt', 'local.txt')# List files
response = s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket='bucket-name')# Delete file
s3.delete_object(Bucket='bucket-name', Key='s3-key.txt')
What’s Coming in Episode 4
Episode 4 covers IAM — Identity and Access Management.
This is how AWS security works. Users, roles, permissions. The most important topic for keeping your AWS account safe.
Follow LearnWithPrashik so you don’t miss it!
